controls.control_title: Informed consent and right to explanation for AI-influenced decisions
controls.control_text_full: People should be fully informed when a decision is informed by or is made on the basis of AI algorithms, including when it affects their safety or human rights, and in those circumstances should have the opportunity to request explanatory information from the relevant AI actor or public sector institutions. In addition, individuals should be able to access the reasons for a decision affecting their rights and freedoms, and have the option of making submissions to a designated staff member of the private sector company or public sector institution able to review and correct the decision.
controls.intent_summary: Individuals must be notified when AI influences decisions affecting their safety or rights, and must be able to request and receive meaningful explanations, plus a path to challenge or correct those decisions through human review.
evidence_signals: Clear notices to users when AI is used in decision-making (e.g., banners, disclosures)
Accessible channels for users to request explanations (e.g., web forms, helplines)
Records of human review processes for contested AI decisions
Designated personnel trained and assigned to handle AI decision appeals
Publicly available procedures for contesting AI-driven outcomes
evidence_anti_signals: No disclosure that AI was used in a decision affecting the user
Explanations provided are technical jargon without actionable insight
No mechanism exists for users to appeal or request correction of AI decisions
Appeals are routed to automated systems without human oversight
controls.remediation_template: Establish clear, accessible mechanisms to notify users when AI influences decisions affecting rights or safety, and provide a defined process for requesting explanations and appealing outcomes via human review.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: AI actors must inform users when AI is used in products or services
controls.control_text_full: AI actors should inform users when a product or service is provided directly or with the assistance of AI systems in a proper and timely manner.
controls.intent_summary: Users must be clearly and timely notified whenever AI is involved in delivering a product or service, regardless of whether the AI is central or auxiliary, to ensure informed interaction and trust.
evidence_signals: User-facing disclosures in apps, websites, or contracts stating AI use
Onboarding flows that include AI disclosure before service activation
FAQs or help sections explicitly detailing AI involvement
Audit trails showing when and how users were notified
Multilingual disclosures for global user bases
evidence_anti_signals: No mention of AI use in marketing, terms of service, or user interfaces
AI use buried in fine print or legal documents without prominence
Disclosures only provided upon request rather than proactively
Use of vague terms like 'smart system' instead of 'AI'
controls.remediation_template: Implement proactive, clear, and timely user notifications whenever AI is used in any capacity within a product or service, ensuring visibility and avoiding obfuscation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Transparency may require sharing code or datasets in cases of serious human rights threats
controls.control_text_full: In cases of serious threats of adverse human rights impacts, transparency may also require the sharing of code or datasets.
controls.intent_summary: When AI systems pose serious risks to human rights, developers and deployers must be prepared to disclose source code or training data to enable independent scrutiny and remediation.
evidence_signals: Code repositories made available under restricted access for auditors or regulators
Data cards or dataset documentation shared with oversight bodies
Legal agreements permitting third-party audit of proprietary models under human rights threat conditions
Public reports citing code or data disclosures made in response to human rights concerns
Ethical impact assessments identifying scenarios requiring code/data disclosure
evidence_anti_signals: Refusal to share code or data even under documented human rights risk
Claims of 'trade secret' used to block all transparency in high-risk contexts
No policy or procedure defined for when code/data disclosure is triggered
Code obfuscation or proprietary lock-in preventing independent verification
controls.remediation_template: Develop and publish a policy that mandates code or dataset disclosure to authorized third parties when AI systems present serious, documented threats to human rights.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Explainability must be meaningful for non-temporary, non-reversible, high-risk AI decisions
controls.control_text_full: In the case of AI applications that impact the end user in a way that is not temporary, easily reversible or otherwise low risk, it should be ensured that the meaningful explanation is provided with any decision that resulted in the action taken in order for the outcome to be considered transparent.
controls.intent_summary: For AI decisions that have lasting, irreversible, or high-stakes consequences, users must receive clear, understandable explanations that allow them to comprehend how and why the decision was made.
evidence_signals: Explanations provided alongside decisions (e.g., loan denials, hiring rejections)
Explanations tailored to non-technical users using plain language and visual aids
Validation that explanations correspond to actual model behavior (e.g., via LIME/SHAP)
User testing confirming comprehension of explanations
Policies defining 'meaningful explanation' criteria for high-risk use cases
evidence_anti_signals: Explanations are only technical feature importance lists without context
No explanation provided for high-stakes decisions like parole or healthcare triage
Explanations are generic and not tied to the specific decision outcome
Explanations only available upon request and delayed beyond reasonable time
controls.remediation_template: Ensure that all high-risk, non-reversible AI decisions include a meaningful, context-specific, and user-understandable explanation delivered at the time of decision.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ultimate responsibility for AI decisions must be attributable to AI actors
controls.control_text_full: The ethical responsibility and liability for the decisions and actions based in any way on an AI system should always ultimately be attributable to AI actors corresponding to their role in the life cycle of the AI system.
controls.intent_summary: No AI system can be held responsible—legal and ethical accountability for its outcomes must always be clearly assigned to human or organizational actors involved in its design, deployment, or operation.
evidence_signals: Organizational charts mapping accountability roles across AI lifecycle stages
Contracts or SLAs specifying liability allocation among developers, vendors, and deployers
Internal policies stating that AI cannot be the final accountable entity
Incident response plans naming responsible parties for AI-related harms
Public statements affirming human accountability for AI outcomes
evidence_anti_signals: Claims that 'the algorithm made the decision' absolving human responsibility
No documented assignment of accountability for AI system failures
Use of third-party AI tools without contractual liability clauses
Lack of audit trails linking decisions to responsible actors
controls.remediation_template: Establish and document clear lines of ethical and legal accountability for all AI system outcomes, ensuring no decision can be attributed solely to the system itself.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Develop oversight, audit, and due diligence mechanisms for AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Appropriate oversight, impact assessment, audit and due diligence mechanisms, including whistle-blowers’ protection, should be developed to ensure accountability for AI systems and their impact throughout their life cycle. Both technical and institutional designs should ensure auditability and traceability of (the working of) AI systems in particular to address any conflicts with human rights norms and standards and threats to environmental and ecosystem well-being.
controls.intent_summary: Organizations must implement formal oversight, auditing, and due diligence processes—including whistleblower protections—to ensure AI systems are traceable, auditable, and aligned with human rights and environmental standards throughout their lifecycle.
evidence_signals: Internal AI audit logs with timestamps and actor attribution
Whistleblower channels specifically for AI ethics concerns
Third-party audit reports on AI systems for human rights compliance
Due diligence checklists for AI procurement and deployment
Technical architectures enabling model and data lineage tracking
evidence_anti_signals: No audit trails for AI decisions or data flows
Whistleblower policies do not mention AI-related concerns
No independent audits conducted on high-risk AI systems
Auditability not considered in system design
No documented due diligence performed before AI deployment
controls.remediation_template: Implement formal, documented oversight and audit mechanisms with whistleblower protections, ensuring full traceability and auditability of AI systems across their lifecycle.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote public awareness and AI literacy through multi-stakeholder education
controls.control_text_full: Public awareness and understanding of AI technologies and the value of data should be promoted through open and accessible education, civic engagement, digital skills and AI ethics training, media and information literacy and training led jointly by governments, intergovernmental organizations, civil society, academia, the media, community leaders and the private sector, and considering the existing linguistic, social and cultural diversity, to ensure effective public participation so that all members of society can take informed decisions about their use of AI systems and be protected from undue influence.
controls.intent_summary: Governments and organizations must collaborate to deliver accessible, culturally appropriate education and training to the public on AI, data, and ethics, empowering citizens to make informed choices and resist manipulation.
evidence_signals: Public AI literacy campaigns with multilingual materials
School or community curricula including AI ethics modules
Partnerships with civil society to deliver training in underserved areas
Media partnerships to disseminate AI risk/benefit information
Surveys measuring public understanding of AI before and after interventions
evidence_anti_signals: No public education initiatives on AI or data rights
Training materials only available in dominant languages or formats
AI literacy confined to technical staff, not general public
No engagement with marginalized or Indigenous communities
Misinformation about AI goes unaddressed by institutions
controls.remediation_template: Launch a multi-stakeholder public education initiative on AI and data rights, tailored to linguistic and cultural diversity, to enable informed societal participation and protection from undue influence.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: AI education must be grounded in human rights and environmental impact
controls.control_text_full: Learning about the impact of AI systems should include learning about, through and for human rights and fundamental freedoms, meaning that the approach and understanding of AI systems should be grounded by their impact on human rights and access to rights, as well as on the environment and ecosystems.
controls.intent_summary: All AI education and training must explicitly connect AI technologies to their real-world impacts on human rights and ecological well-being, not just technical performance.
evidence_signals: Curricula that link AI use cases to human rights frameworks (e.g., UDHR, CRC)
Case studies on AI’s environmental footprint (e.g., energy use, e-waste)
Training modules on AI’s role in discrimination, surveillance, or climate injustice
Educational materials referencing UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights
Student projects evaluating AI systems through human rights and ecological lenses
evidence_anti_signals: AI training focused solely on accuracy, speed, or efficiency metrics
No mention of human rights or environmental impacts in educational materials
Ethics modules treated as optional add-ons rather than core content
Training materials ignore impacts on marginalized communities or ecosystems
controls.remediation_template: Integrate human rights and environmental impact analysis as mandatory components in all AI education, training, and public awareness programs.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Implement ethical impact assessments (EIA) for AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States should introduce frameworks for impact assessments, such as ethical impact assessment, to identify and assess benefits, concerns and risks of AI systems, as well as appropriate risk prevention, mitigation and monitoring measures, among other assurance mechanisms. Such impact assessments should identify impacts on human rights and fundamental freedoms, in particular but not limited to the rights of marginalized and vulnerable people or people in vulnerable situations, labour rights, the environment and ecosystems and ethical and social implications, and facilitate citizen participation in line with the values and principles set forth in this Recommendation.
controls.intent_summary: All public and high-risk private AI systems must undergo formal ethical impact assessments that evaluate human rights, environmental, and social risks, with meaningful public input, before deployment.
evidence_signals: Published EIA templates or checklists aligned with UNESCO guidance
EIA reports for deployed AI systems, including stakeholder consultation records
Risk mitigation plans tied to EIA findings
Public comment periods or participatory forums for EIA drafts
EIA results used to block or modify AI system deployment
evidence_anti_signals: No EIA conducted prior to AI deployment
EIA limited to technical performance metrics only
No documentation of consultation with affected communities
EIA reports are confidential and never made public
EIA treated as a box-ticking exercise without real analysis
controls.remediation_template: Mandate and publish a standardized Ethical Impact Assessment process for all high-risk AI systems, requiring evaluation of human rights, environmental, and social impacts with documented public consultation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Implement due diligence and monitoring for AI systems’ human rights and socio-economic impacts
controls.control_text_full: Member States and private sector companies should develop due diligence and oversight mechanisms to identify, prevent, mitigate and account for how they address the impact of AI systems on the respect for human rights, rule of law and inclusive societies. Member States should also be able to assess the socio-economic impact of AI systems on poverty and ensure that the gap between people living in wealth and poverty, as well as the digital divide among and within countries, are not increased with the massive adoption of AI technologies at present and in the future.
controls.intent_summary: Organizations must conduct ongoing due diligence to detect and mitigate AI’s negative impacts on human rights, social equity, and economic inclusion, and actively prevent AI from worsening poverty or digital divides.
evidence_signals: Human rights due diligence reports for AI deployments
Monitoring metrics tracking AI’s impact on poverty, employment, or digital access
Bias audits conducted on AI systems used in public services
Policies to prevent AI from exacerbating the digital divide
Public dashboards showing AI’s socio-economic impact indicators
evidence_anti_signals: No monitoring of AI’s impact on poverty or inequality
Due diligence focused only on legal compliance, not equity
AI systems deployed in public services without assessing differential impacts
No data collected on how AI affects marginalized groups
Digital access gaps widened without mitigation plans
controls.remediation_template: Establish ongoing due diligence and monitoring processes to assess and mitigate AI’s impact on human rights, poverty, and digital inclusion, with public reporting on outcomes.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure inclusive, multi-stakeholder participation in AI governance
controls.control_text_full: Participation of different stakeholders throughout the AI system life cycle is necessary for inclusive approaches to AI governance, enabling the benefits to be shared by all, and to contribute to sustainable development. Stakeholders include but are not limited to governments, intergovernmental organizations, the technical community, civil society, researchers and academia, media, education, policy-makers, private sector companies, human rights institutions and equality bodies, anti-discrimination monitoring bodies, and groups for youth and children. The adoption of open standards and interoperability to facilitate collaboration should be in place. Measures should be adopted to take into account shifts in technologies, the emergence of new groups of stakeholders, and to allow for meaningful participation by marginalized groups, communities and individuals and, where relevant, in the case of Indigenous Peoples, respect for the self-governance of their data.
controls.intent_summary: AI governance must actively include diverse stakeholders—including marginalized groups and Indigenous communities—throughout the AI lifecycle, with respect for their autonomy, especially over their data, and through open, interoperable systems.
evidence_signals: Stakeholder engagement plans listing participants by category
Documentation of consultation with Indigenous communities on data governance
Inclusion of civil society and youth representatives in AI oversight bodies
Use of open standards and APIs to enable third-party interoperability
Policies affirming Indigenous data sovereignty in AI projects
evidence_anti_signals: Stakeholder lists exclude marginalized groups or Indigenous communities
No mechanism for public or civil society input in AI design or deployment
Closed, proprietary systems preventing third-party audit or integration
Data collected from Indigenous communities without consent or self-governance
Decision-making bodies composed solely of tech companies or government officials
controls.remediation_template: Design and implement a structured, inclusive stakeholder engagement process for AI governance that ensures meaningful participation from marginalized groups, civil society, and Indigenous communities, with respect for their data self-governance.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Implement enforceable transparency protocols and assess socio-economic impact of AI systems
controls.control_text_full: ight mechanisms to identify, prevent, mitigate and account for how they address the impact of AI systems on the respect for human rights, rule of law and inclusive societies. Member States should also be able to assess the socio -economic impact of AI systems on poverty and ensure that the gap between people living in wealth and poverty, as well as the digital divide among and within countries, are not increased with the massive adoptio n of AI technologies at present and in the future. In order to do this, in particular, enforceable transparency protocols should be implemented, corresponding to the access to information, including information of public interest held by private entities. Member States, private sector companies and civil society should investigate the sociological and psychological effects of AI-based recommendations on humans in their decision -making autonomy. AI systems identified as potential risks to human rights should be broadly tested by AI actors, including in real-world conditions if needed, as part of the Ethical Impact Assessment, before releasing them in the market.
controls.intent_summary: Governments and AI actors must implement transparent protocols to access information held by private entities and assess AI's socio-economic impacts to prevent widening inequality and digital divides. AI systems posing human rights risks must be tested in real-world conditions as part of an Ethical Impact Assessment before deployment.
evidence_signals: Published transparency protocols for public interest data access
Socio-economic impact assessments showing no increase in poverty or digital divide
Documentation of real-world testing for high-risk AI systems
Reports on sociological/psychological effects of AI recommendations
Ethical Impact Assessment records prior to market release
evidence_anti_signals: No public access to data held by private AI providers
Absence of pre-deployment testing for high-risk systems
Lack of analysis on AI's impact on poverty or digital inclusion
controls.remediation_template: Implement enforceable transparency protocols for public interest data and conduct real-world ethical impact assessments for high-risk AI systems before deployment.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Monitor AI system life cycle with ethical impact assessments aligned with human rights law
controls.control_text_full: Member States and business enterprises should implement appropriate measures to monitor all phases of an AI system life cycle, including the functioning of algorithms used for decision-making, the data, as well as AI actors involved in the process, especially in public services and where direct end -user interaction is needed, as part of ethical imp act assessment. Member States’ human rights law obligations should form part of the ethical aspects of AI system assessments.
controls.intent_summary: All AI actors must continuously monitor every phase of AI systems—data, algorithms, and actors—especially in public services, integrating human rights law obligations into ethical impact assessments.
evidence_signals: Life cycle monitoring logs for AI systems in public services
Ethical impact assessments referencing national human rights law
Audit trails of algorithmic decision-making processes
Documentation of end-user interaction monitoring
Human rights compliance checklists integrated into AI development
evidence_anti_signals: No monitoring of algorithmic behavior during operation
Ethical assessments that omit human rights law references
Absence of records for end-user interaction in public AI systems
controls.remediation_template: Integrate human rights law obligations into continuous life cycle monitoring of AI systems, especially in public services and end-user-facing applications.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Adopt regulatory framework for ethical impact assessments with oversight mechanisms
controls.control_text_full: Governments should adopt a regulatory framework that sets out a procedure, particularly for public authorities, to carry out e thical impact assessments on AI systems to predict consequences, mitigate risks, avoid harmful consequences, facilitate citizen participation and address societal challenges. The assessment should also establish appropriate oversight mechanisms, including auditability, traceability and explainability, which enable the assessment of algorithms, data and design processes, as well as include external review of AI systems. Ethical impact assessments should be transparent and open to the public, where appropriat e. Such assessments should also be multidisciplinary, multi - stakeholder, multicultural, pluralistic and inclusive. The public authorities should be required to monitor the AI systems implemented and/or deployed by those authorities by introducing appropriate mechanisms and tools.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must establish mandatory regulatory procedures for ethical impact assessments that include auditability, traceability, explainability, public access, and external review—ensuring assessments are inclusive and ongoing for public AI systems.
evidence_signals: Regulatory framework document mandating ethical impact assessments
Publicly accessible ethical impact assessment reports
Evidence of external audits or reviews of AI systems
Multidisciplinary assessment teams documented in reports
Tools or dashboards for monitoring public AI deployments
evidence_anti_signals: No formal regulatory procedure for ethical assessments
Assessments closed to public scrutiny
Lack of external review or audit trails
controls.remediation_template: Establish a mandatory regulatory framework for ethical impact assessments that includes auditability, traceability, explainability, public access, and external review for all public AI systems.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure inclusive, transparent, and multi-stakeholder AI governance mechanisms
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that AI governance mechanisms are inclusive, transparent, multidisciplinary, multilateral (this includes the possibility of mitigation and redress of harm across borders) and multi -stakeholder. In particular, governance should include aspects of anticipation, and effective protection, monitoring of impact, enforcement and redress.
controls.intent_summary: AI governance must be inclusive, transparent, and involve multiple stakeholders across borders, with built-in mechanisms for anticipating harm, protecting rights, monitoring impact, enforcing rules, and providing redress.
evidence_signals: Governance charter listing multi-stakeholder participants
Publicly documented processes for harm redress across borders
Evidence of multidisciplinary governance committees
Anticipatory risk assessment frameworks in governance plans
Public reports on monitoring and enforcement activities
evidence_anti_signals: Governance bodies composed solely of government or corporate reps
No mechanism for cross-border harm redress
Absence of anticipatory or monitoring components in governance
controls.remediation_template: Design AI governance mechanisms that are inclusive, transparent, multidisciplinary, and multilateral—with clear processes for anticipation, protection, monitoring, enforcement, and cross-border redress.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Implement enforcement and remedial mechanisms for AI-related harms
controls.control_text_full: Member States and business enterprises should implement appropriate measures to monitor all phases of an AI system life cycle, including the functioning of algorithms used for decision-making, the data, as well as AI actors involved in the process, especially in public services and where direct end -user interaction is needed, as part of ethical imp act assessment. Member States’ human rights law obligations should form part of the ethical aspects of AI system assessments. 53. Governments should adopt a regulatory framework that sets out a procedure, particularly for public authorities, to carry out e thical impact assessments on AI systems to predict consequences, mitigate risks, avoid harmful consequences, facilitate citizen participation and address societal challenges. The assessment should also establish appropriate oversight mechanisms, including auditability, traceability and explainability, which enable the assessment of algorithms, data and design processes, as well as include external review of AI systems. Ethical impact assessments should be transparent and open to the public, where appropriat e. Such assessments should also be multidisciplinary, multi - stakeholder, multicultural, pluralistic and inclusive. The public authorities should be required to monitor the AI systems implemented and/or deployed by those authorities by introducing appropriate mechanisms and tools. POLICY AREA 2: ETHICAL GOVERNANCE AND STEWARDSHIP 54. Member States should ensure that AI governance mechanisms are inclusive, transparent, multidisciplinary, multilateral (this includes the possibility of mitigation and redress of harm across borders) and multi -stakeholder. In particular, governance should include aspects of anticipation, and effective protection, monitoring of impact, enforcement and redress. 55. Member States should ensure that harms caused through AI systems are investigated and redressed, by enacting strong enforcement mechanisms and remedial actions, to make certain that human rights and fundamental freedoms and the rule of law are respected in the digital world and in the physical world. Such mechanisms and ac tions should include remediation mechanisms provided by private and public sector companies. The auditability and traceability of AI systems should be promoted to this end. In addition, Member States should strengthen their institutional capacities to deli ver on this commitment and should collaborate with researchers and other stakeholders to investigate, prevent and mitigate any potentially malicious uses of AI systems.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must enact strong enforcement and remedial mechanisms to investigate and redress AI-caused harms, ensuring accountability through auditability and traceability, and collaborate with stakeholders to prevent malicious AI use.
evidence_signals: Formalized remediation procedures for AI harms
Public reports of investigations into AI-related harms
Audit trails and traceability logs for AI decisions
Collaborative initiatives with researchers on malicious AI
Legal or administrative penalties for non-compliance
evidence_anti_signals: No formal process to report or remedy AI harms
Absence of audit or traceability in AI systems
No collaboration with researchers on malicious AI use
controls.remediation_template: Establish enforceable mechanisms to investigate and remedy AI-caused harms, ensuring auditability and traceability, and collaborate with researchers to prevent malicious applications.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Develop certification mechanisms for AI systems that balance ethics and innovation
controls.control_text_full: Member States are encouraged to develop national and regional AI strategies and to consider forms of soft governance such as a certification mechanism for AI systems and the mutual recognition of their certification, according to the sensitivity of the application domain and expected impact on human rights, the environment and ecosystems , and other ethical considerations set forth in this Recommendation. Such a mechanism might include different levels of audit of systems, data, and adherence to ethical guidelines and to procedural requirements in view of ethical aspects. At the same time, such a mechanism should not hinder innovation or disadvantage small and medium enterprises or start -ups, civil society as well as research and science organizations, as a result of an excessive administrative burden. These mechanisms should also include a regular monitoring component to ensure system robustness and continued integrity and adherence to ethical guidelines over the entire life cycle of the AI system, requiring re-certification if necessary.
controls.intent_summary: Member States should create tiered, risk-based certification systems for AI systems that ensure ethical compliance across the life cycle, avoid burdening small actors, and require re-certification to maintain integrity.
evidence_signals: National AI certification framework with risk tiers
Evidence of mutual recognition agreements between jurisdictions
Documentation of re-certification processes
Exemptions or simplified processes for SMEs and NGOs
Regular monitoring logs for certified systems
evidence_anti_signals: One-size-fits-all certification with no risk-based tiers
Certification process excludes SMEs or civil society
No re-certification or ongoing monitoring requirement
controls.remediation_template: Develop a tiered, risk-based AI certification system with mutual recognition, re-certification requirements, and safeguards to avoid burdening small actors.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Conduct transparent self-assessments of AI adoption for human rights compliance
controls.control_text_full: Member States and public authorities should carr y out transparent self -assessment of existing and proposed AI systems, which, in particular, should include the assessment of whether the adoption of AI is appropriate and, if so, should include further assessment to determine what the appropriate method is, as well as assessment as to whether such adoption would result in violations or abuses of Member States’ human rights law obligations, and if that is the case, prohibit its use.
controls.intent_summary: Public authorities must perform transparent self-assessments to determine if AI adoption is appropriate and compliant with human rights law—and prohibit use if it would violate those rights.
evidence_signals: Published self-assessment reports for AI systems
Decision logs showing prohibition of non-compliant AI systems
Human rights compliance checklists used in assessments
Publicly accessible rationale for AI adoption or rejection
Internal review boards documenting assessment outcomes
evidence_anti_signals: No documented self-assessments for AI systems
AI deployed without human rights impact evaluation
Assessments kept confidential or inaccessible
controls.remediation_template: Require public authorities to conduct and publish transparent self-assessments of AI systems, prohibiting deployment if human rights violations are identified.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Appoint independent AI Ethics Officers and establish a global network
controls.control_text_full: Member States should encourage public entities, private sector companie s and civil society organizations to involve different stakeholders in their AI governance and to consider [[PAGE_17]] adding the role of an independent AI Ethics Officer or some other mechanism to oversee ethical impact assessment, auditing and continuous monitoring efforts and ensure ethical guidance of AI systems. Member States, private sector companies and civil society organizations, with the support of UNESCO, are encouraged to create a network of independent AI Ethics Officers to give support to this process at national, regional and international levels.
controls.intent_summary: Organizations should appoint independent AI Ethics Officers to oversee ethical assessments and monitoring, and Member States should support a global network of such officers to strengthen ethical governance.
evidence_signals: Job descriptions or appointments of AI Ethics Officers
Publicly listed AI Ethics Officer contact or reporting structure
Evidence of a national or international network of officers
Ethics Officer involvement in audit or assessment reports
Training or accreditation programs for Ethics Officers
evidence_anti_signals: No designated AI Ethics Officer role
Ethics role held by internal legal or compliance staff without independence
No evidence of cross-border coordination among officers
controls.remediation_template: Appoint independent AI Ethics Officers to oversee ethical assessments and monitoring, and support the creation of a global network for coordination and capacity building.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Develop a national digital ecosystem for ethical and inclusive AI development
controls.control_text_full: Member States should foster the development of, and access to, a digital ecosystem for ethical and inclusive development of AI systems at the national level, including to address gaps in access to the AI sys tem life cycle, while contributing to international collaboration. Such an ecosystem includes, in particular, digital technologies and infrastructure, and mechanisms for sharing AI knowledge, as appropriate.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must build national digital ecosystems that ensure equitable access to AI development tools and knowledge, addressing gaps across the AI life cycle and supporting global collaboration.
evidence_signals: National AI infrastructure roadmap
Publicly accessible AI datasets or model repositories
Knowledge-sharing platforms for AI development
Programs to bridge access gaps for marginalized communities
Evidence of international data or tool-sharing partnerships
evidence_anti_signals: No public AI infrastructure or knowledge-sharing platforms
AI development tools restricted to urban or elite institutions
No efforts to address access gaps in rural or marginalized areas
controls.remediation_template: Develop a national digital ecosystem with accessible infrastructure and knowledge-sharing mechanisms to ensure inclusive participation across the AI life cycle.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure inclusive participation of LMICs in international AI governance
controls.control_text_full: Member States should establish mechanisms, in collaboration with international organizations, transnational corporations, academic institutions and civil society, to ensure the active participation of all Member States, especially LMICs, in particular LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, in international discussi ons concerning AI governance. This can be through the provision of funds, ensuring equal regional participation, or any other mechanisms. Furthermore, in order to ensure the inclusiveness of AI fora, Member States should facilitate the travel of AI actors in and out of their territory, especially from LMICs, in particular LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, for the purpose of participating in these fora.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must actively enable participation of low-income and vulnerable countries in global AI governance forums through funding, travel support, and equitable representation mechanisms.
evidence_signals: Funding programs for LMIC participation in AI fora
Travel grants or visas facilitated for AI actors from LDCs/LLDCs/SIDS
Equitable regional representation in AI governance bodies
Public reports on participation rates by country income level
Partnerships with academic institutions in LMICs for AI policy input
evidence_anti_signals: No financial or logistical support for LMIC participants
AI fora dominated by high-income countries
No tracking of regional participation metrics
controls.remediation_template: Establish funding, travel, and representation mechanisms to ensure active and equitable participation of LMICs, especially LDCs, LLDCs, and SIDS, in international AI governance forums.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure national AI legislation complies with human rights law and promotes rights throughout the life cycle
controls.control_text_full: Amendments to the existing or elaboration of new national legislation addressing AI systems must comply with Member States’ human rights law obligations and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms throughout the AI system life cycle. Promotion thereof should also take the form of governance initiatives, good exemplars of collaborative practices regarding AI systems, and national and international technical and methodological guidelines as AI technologies advance. Diverse sectors, including the private sector, in their practices regarding AI systems must respect, protect and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms using existing and new instruments in combination with this Recommendation.
controls.intent_summary: All national AI legislation must align with human rights law and actively promote rights across the AI life cycle, with private and public sectors using both existing and new tools to uphold these obligations.
evidence_signals: Legislative text explicitly referencing human rights law
AI laws with life cycle-wide rights protections
Publicly listed good practice examples of rights-respecting AI
Private sector compliance reports aligned with Recommendation
Technical guidelines integrating human rights standards
evidence_anti_signals: AI laws that omit human rights references
Legislation focused only on efficiency or innovation, not rights
Private sector practices contradicting human rights obligations
controls.remediation_template: Ensure all national AI legislation explicitly complies with human rights law and promotes rights throughout the AI system life cycle, with public and private sectors aligned to this standard.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Monitor social and economic impact of AI in human rights-sensitive sectors
controls.control_text_full: Member States that acquire Al systems for human rights -sensitive use cases, such as law enforcement, welfare, employment, media and information providers, health care and the independent judiciary system should provide mechanisms to monitor the social and economic impact of such systems by appropriate oversight authorities, including independent data protection authorities, sectoral oversight and public bodies responsible for oversight.
controls.intent_summary: Where AI is used in human rights-sensitive sectors (e.g., law enforcement, healthcare), governments must establish dedicated oversight mechanisms to monitor social and economic impacts through independent authorities.
evidence_signals: Designated oversight bodies for AI in healthcare or law enforcement
Reports on social/economic impact assessments in sensitive sectors
Independent data protection authority involvement in AI audits
Sector-specific monitoring frameworks for AI
Publicly accessible oversight reports
evidence_anti_signals: No oversight body assigned to high-risk AI sectors
Impact monitoring absent in law enforcement or welfare AI
Oversight conducted only internally without independence
controls.remediation_template: Establish independent oversight mechanisms to monitor social and economic impacts of AI systems in human rights-sensitive sectors such as law enforcement, healthcare, and welfare.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Enhance judicial capacity and ensure human oversight in AI-assisted justice
controls.control_text_full: Member States should enhance the capacity of the judiciary to make decisions related to AI systems as per the rule of law and in line with international law and standards, including in the use of AI systems in their deliberations, while ensuring that the principle of human oversight is upheld. In case AI systems are used by the judiciary, sufficient safeguards are needed to guarantee inter alia the protection of fundamental human rights, the rule of law, judicial independence as well as the principle of human oversight, and to ensure a trustworthy, public interest-oriented and human-centric development and use of AI systems in the judiciary.
controls.intent_summary: Judiciaries must be trained to use AI in compliance with human rights and rule of law, with mandatory human oversight and safeguards to preserve judicial independence and public trust.
evidence_signals: Judicial training programs on AI and human rights
Court policies requiring human review of AI-generated decisions
Legal rulings referencing human oversight in AI-assisted cases
Safeguards documented for AI use in judicial deliberations
Public reports on AI use in judiciary with transparency measures
evidence_anti_signals: AI used in judicial decisions without human review
No judicial training on AI ethics or rights
No documented safeguards for judicial independence
controls.remediation_template: Train judicial personnel on AI use, mandate human oversight in AI-assisted decisions, and implement safeguards to protect judicial independence and human rights.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Develop international safety and transparency standards for AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that governments and multilateral organizations play a leading ro le in ensuring the safety and security of AI systems, with multi -stakeholder participation. Specifically, Member States, international organizations and other relevant bodies should develop international standards that describe measurable, testable levels of safety and transparency, so that systems can be objectively assessed and levels of compliance determined. Furthermore, Member States and business enterprises should continuously support strategic research on potential safety and security risks of AI technologies and should encourage research into transparency and explainability, inclusion and literacy by putting additional funding into those areas for different domains and at different levels, such as technical and natural language.
controls.intent_summary: Governments and international bodies must develop measurable, testable global standards for AI safety and transparency, and fund research into explainability, inclusion, and literacy across technical and linguistic domains.
evidence_signals: Published international AI safety and transparency standards
Funding allocations for explainability and inclusion research
Test protocols for measuring AI transparency levels
Research grants targeting natural language explainability
Public reports on AI safety risk assessments
evidence_anti_signals: No international standards for AI safety or transparency
No funding allocated to explainability or literacy research
Safety assessments based on vague or untestable criteria
controls.remediation_template: Develop and adopt measurable international standards for AI safety and transparency, and increase funding for research into explainability, inclusion, and literacy across domains.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure AI actor actions align with international human rights law and cultural contexts
controls.control_text_full: Member States should implement policies to ensure that the actions of AI actors are consistent with international human rights law, standards and principles throughout the life cycle of AI systems, while taking into full consideration the current cultural and social diversities, including local customs and religious traditions, with due regard to the precedence and universality of human rights.
controls.intent_summary: All AI actors must comply with international human rights law throughout the AI life cycle, while respecting cultural and social diversity—without compromising the universality of human rights.
evidence_signals: Policies requiring human rights compliance for AI actors
Cultural sensitivity assessments in AI design processes
Documentation of human rights precedence in AI deployment decisions
Training materials on universal human rights in local contexts
Audit reports verifying alignment with international standards
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems designed without reference to human rights law
Cultural justifications used to override fundamental rights
No documentation of human rights precedence in decision-making
controls.remediation_template: Implement policies ensuring all AI actors comply with international human rights law throughout the life cycle, respecting cultural contexts without undermining universal rights.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Require disclosure and combat of stereotyping and biased data in AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States should put in place mechanisms to require AI actors to disclose and combat any kind of stereotyping in the outco mes of AI systems and data, whether by design or by negligence, and to ensure that training data sets for AI systems do not foster cultural, economic or social inequalities, prejudice, the spreading of disinformation and misinformation, and disruption of freedom of expression and access to information. Particular attention should be given to regions where the data are scarce.
controls.intent_summary: AI actors must disclose and actively combat stereotyping and bias in AI outcomes and training data, especially in data-scarce regions, to prevent reinforcing inequalities, prejudice, and misinformation.
evidence_signals: Bias disclosure reports for AI systems
Data auditing protocols for stereotyping and prejudice
Training datasets documented with provenance and diversity metrics
Mitigation actions taken against biased outputs
Special attention documented for data-scarce regions
evidence_anti_signals: No disclosure of bias or stereotyping in AI outputs
Training data with known demographic gaps and no mitigation
No monitoring for disinformation or misinformation in outputs
controls.remediation_template: Require AI actors to disclose and mitigate stereotyping and bias in outcomes and training data, with special attention to data-scarce regions, to prevent reinforcing inequality and misinformation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote diversity and inclusion in AI development teams and datasets
controls.control_text_full: Member States should implement policies to promote and increase diversity and inclusiveness that reflect their populations in AI development teams and training datasets, and to ensure equal access to AI technologies and their benefits, particularly for marginalized groups, both from rural and urban zones.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must ensure AI development teams and training datasets reflect population diversity, and guarantee equitable access to AI benefits for marginalized groups in both rural and urban areas.
evidence_signals: Diversity metrics for AI development teams
Training datasets with demographic representation benchmarks
Programs to increase access for rural or marginalized communities
Public reports on equitable AI benefit distribution
Inclusion targets in national AI strategies
evidence_anti_signals: AI teams homogenous in gender, ethnicity, or geography
Training data lacking representation of marginalized groups
No programs to improve access for rural or low-income populations
controls.remediation_template: Implement policies requiring diverse AI development teams and representative training datasets, and ensure equitable access to AI benefits for marginalized and rural populations.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Establish accountability frameworks with human oversight and prohibit AI legal personality
controls.control_text_full: Member States should develop, review and adapt, as appropriate, regulator y frameworks to achieve accountability and responsibility for the content and outcomes of AI systems at the different phases of their life cycle. Member States should, where necessary, introduce liability frameworks or clarify the interpretation of existing frameworks to ensure the attribution of accountability for the outcomes and the functioning of AI systems. Furthermore, when developing regulatory frameworks, Member States should, in particular, take into account that ultimate responsibility and account ability must always lie with natural or legal persons and that AI systems should not be given legal personality themselves. To ensure this, such regulatory frameworks should be consistent with the principle of human oversight and establish a comprehensive approach focused on AI actors and the technological processes involved across the different stages
controls.intent_summary: Member States must create accountability and liability frameworks that assign responsibility to human or legal persons—not AI systems—and ensure human oversight is embedded across all stages of the AI life cycle.
evidence_signals: Liability laws assigning responsibility to human actors
Regulatory text explicitly prohibiting AI legal personality
Human oversight requirements documented in AI deployment policies
Audit trails linking AI decisions to responsible persons
Comprehensive frameworks covering all AI life cycle stages
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems treated as legally responsible entities
No clear assignment of liability for AI outcomes
Human oversight absent in high-stakes AI applications
controls.remediation_template: Establish clear accountability and liability frameworks that assign responsibility to human or legal persons, prohibit AI legal personality, and embed human oversight across all AI life cycle stages.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Involve all AI actors in norm-setting and use regulatory sandboxes
controls.control_text_full: In order to establish norms where these do not exist, or to adapt the existing legal frameworks, Member States should involve all AI actors (including, but not limited to, researchers, representatives of civil society and law enforcement, insurers, investors, manufacturers, engineers, lawyers and users). The norms can mature into best practices, laws and regulations. Member States are further encouraged to use mechanisms such as policy prototypes and regulatory sandboxes to accelerate the development of laws, regulations and policies, including regular reviews thereof, in line with the rapid development of new technologies and ensure that laws and regulations can be tested in a safe environment before being officially adopted. Member States should support local governments in the development of local policies, regulations and laws in line with national and international legal frameworks.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must engage diverse stakeholders in co-creating AI governance norms and use safe testing environments like sandboxes to iteratively refine regulations before full implementation, ensuring adaptability and inclusivity.
evidence_signals: Publicly documented stakeholder consultation records with diverse AI actors
Regulatory sandbox programs with published test results and outcomes
Local government AI policy templates aligned with national frameworks
Annual review cycles for AI regulations based on sandbox feedback
Multi-stakeholder advisory boards with civil society and industry representation
evidence_anti_signals: Regulations developed without public or industry input
No mechanism for testing AI rules before enforcement
Absence of local policy support or alignment with national frameworks
Regulatory updates occurring only in response to crises, not proactively
controls.remediation_template: Establish a formal multi-stakeholder process for AI regulation development and implement at least one regulatory sandbox with public reporting on outcomes and iterative policy updates.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Require transparency and explainability in AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States should set clear requirements for AI system transparency and explainability so as to help ensure the trustworthiness of the full AI system life cycle. Such requirements should involve the design and implementation of impact mechanisms that take into consideration the nature of application domain, intended use, target audience and feasibility of each particular AI system.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must mandate that AI systems are designed to be transparent and explainable, with impact assessments tailored to the specific context, use case, and users to build trust and enable accountability.
evidence_signals: Model cards or documentation requiring explainability metrics
User-facing explanations for AI decisions in high-risk domains
Impact assessments that evaluate transparency by domain and audience
Audit requirements for algorithmic decision logs
Certification standards for explainability in public sector AI
evidence_anti_signals: Black-box AI deployed in public services without documentation
No user-accessible explanations for consequential AI decisions
Impact assessments that ignore target audience or domain context
Procurement contracts that do not require transparency documentation
controls.remediation_template: Mandate that all high-risk AI systems include documented transparency and explainability features tailored to their application domain, target users, and feasibility, with verification through third-party audits.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure continual evaluation of training data quality
controls.control_text_full: Member States should work to develop data governance strategies that ensure the continual evaluation of the quality of training data for AI systems including the adequacy of the data collection and selection processes, proper data security and protection measures, as well as feedback mechanisms to learn from mistakes and share best practices among all AI actors.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must require ongoing assessment of training data quality, including how data is collected, secured, and improved through feedback loops, to ensure reliability and ethical integrity of AI systems.
evidence_signals: Data quality assessment reports updated quarterly
Documented data collection protocols with bias checks
Feedback loops for reporting data errors or harms
Public repositories of best practices for data governance
Audit trails showing data lineage and provenance
evidence_anti_signals: No documented process for evaluating training data quality
Data sources undisclosed or unverified
No mechanism to report or correct harmful data patterns
Training data used without consent or legal basis
controls.remediation_template: Implement a mandatory data governance framework requiring continuous evaluation of training data quality, documented collection methods, security protocols, and feedback mechanisms for error correction.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Protect privacy through legislative frameworks and privacy by design
controls.control_text_full: Member States should put in place appropriate safeguards to protect the right to privacy in accordance with international law, including addressing concerns such as surveillance. Member States should, among other s, adopt or enforce legislative frameworks that provide appropriate protection, compliant with international law. Member States should strongly encourage all AI actors, including business enterprises, to follow existing international standards and, in particular, to carry out adequate privacy impact assessments, as part of ethical impact assessments, which take into account the wider socio-economic impact of the intended data processing, and to apply privacy by design in their systems. Privacy should be respected, protected and promoted throughout the life cycle of AI systems.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must enforce privacy laws aligned with international standards, require privacy impact assessments for AI systems, and mandate privacy-by-design principles to protect individuals throughout the AI lifecycle.
evidence_signals: Privacy impact assessments (PIAs) conducted for all high-risk AI systems
Privacy-by-design architecture documented in system specs
Legislation prohibiting mass surveillance via AI
Publicly available PIA reports for government AI deployments
Certification requirements for privacy-compliant AI tools
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems deployed without privacy impact assessments
No privacy-by-design requirements in procurement policies
Surveillance applications using AI without legal basis
Data processing without informed consent or anonymization
controls.remediation_template: Require all AI systems handling personal data to undergo privacy impact assessments, implement privacy-by-design principles, and comply with international privacy standards, with enforcement through legal frameworks.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure individual rights over personal data in AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that individuals retain rights over their personal data and are protected by a framework, which notably foresees: transparency; appropriate safeguards for the processing of sensitive data; an appropriate level of data protection; effective and meaningful accountability schemes and mechanisms; the full enjoyment of the data subjects’ rights and the ability to access and erase their personal data in AI systems, except for certain circumstances in compliance with international law; an appropriate level of protection in full compliance with data protection legislation where data are being used for commercial purposes such as enabling micro-targeted advertising, transferred cross-border; and an effective independent oversight as part of a data governance mechanism which keeps individuals in control of their personal data and fosters the benefits of a free flow of information internationally, including access to data.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must guarantee individuals full control over their personal data in AI systems, including rights to access, erase, and object to processing, with independent oversight and compliance with cross-border data protection laws.
evidence_signals: Mechanisms for individuals to request access or deletion of AI-held data
Independent data protection authority overseeing AI systems
Cross-border data transfer agreements compliant with GDPR or equivalent
Public dashboards showing data usage for micro-targeting
Audit logs of data subject rights requests and responses
evidence_anti_signals: No mechanism for individuals to delete data from AI systems
Cross-border data transfers without adequacy decisions
Micro-targeting without user consent or transparency
No independent oversight body for AI data practices
controls.remediation_template: Establish legal rights for individuals to access, correct, and erase their personal data in AI systems, mandate independent oversight, and enforce compliance with international data protection standards for cross-border transfers.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure full security for sensitive personal data
controls.control_text_full: Member States should establish their data policies or equivalent frameworks, or reinforce existing ones, to ensure full security for personal data and sensitive data, which, if disclosed, may cause exceptional damage, injury or hardship to individuals. Examples include data relating to offences, criminal proceedings and convictions, and related security measures; biometric, genetic and health data; and -personal data such as that relating to race, colour, descent, gender, age, language, religion, political opinion, national origin, ethnic origin, social origin, economic or social condition of birth, or disability and any other characteristics.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must implement enhanced security measures for sensitive personal data—such as biometric, health, or identity-related information—to prevent harm from misuse or breaches in AI systems.
evidence_signals: Classification of data types by sensitivity level in data policies
Encryption and access controls for biometric and health data
Audit logs for access to sensitive data in AI systems
Prohibitions on using race, religion, or disability data in high-risk AI
Security certification requirements for systems handling sensitive data
evidence_anti_signals: Use of biometric or health data without explicit legal basis
No distinction between sensitive and non-sensitive data in AI training
Publicly accessible datasets containing protected characteristics
No encryption or access controls for sensitive data in AI pipelines
controls.remediation_template: Enact strict data security requirements for sensitive personal data in AI systems, prohibit unjustified use of protected characteristics, and mandate encryption, access controls, and audit trails.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote open data and public data sharing mechanisms
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote open data. In this regard, Member States should consider reviewing their policies and regulatory frameworks, including on access to information and open government to reflect AI-specific requirements and promoting mechanisms, such as open repositories for publicly funded or publicly held data and source code and data trusts, to support the safe, fair, legal and ethical sharing of data, among others.
controls.intent_summary: Governments should encourage open access to publicly funded or held data and source code through secure repositories and data trusts to enable ethical, transparent, and equitable AI development.
evidence_signals: Publicly accessible open data repositories for AI training
Mandated open-source release of government-funded AI models
Data trust frameworks with governance boards and access rules
Open government portals with AI-relevant datasets
Policies requiring open licensing for publicly funded AI outputs
evidence_anti_signals: All government-funded AI models kept proprietary
No public access to datasets used in public AI systems
Data sharing restricted without justification
No mechanisms for third parties to audit or reuse public data
controls.remediation_template: Create and maintain open repositories for publicly funded AI datasets and source code, and establish data trust frameworks to enable ethical, secure, and equitable data sharing.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote quality, robust, and diverse datasets with annotation standards
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote and facilitate the use of quality and robust datasets for training, development and use of AI systems, and exercise vigilance in overseeing their collection and use. This could, if possible and feasible, include investing in the creation of gold standard datasets, including open and trustworthy datasets, which are diverse, constructed on a valid legal basis, including consent of data subjects, when required by law. Standards for annotating datasets should be encouraged, including disaggregating data on gender and other bases, so it can easily be determined how a dataset is gathered and what properties it has.
controls.intent_summary: Governments should support the creation of high-quality, diverse, and legally compliant datasets with standardized annotations—including disaggregation by gender and other attributes—to ensure fairness and traceability in AI training.
evidence_signals: Gold standard datasets publicly available with metadata
Annotation guidelines requiring disaggregation by gender, race, etc.
Legal basis documentation for data collection (e.g., consent logs)
Dataset cards detailing collection methods and limitations
Public audits of dataset diversity and representativeness
evidence_anti_signals: Datasets with no documentation on collection or annotation
No disaggregation of data by protected characteristics
Training data sourced without consent or legal basis
Datasets lacking diversity metrics or bias assessments
controls.remediation_template: Develop and mandate dataset standards requiring diversity metrics, disaggregation by protected attributes, legal basis documentation, and standardized metadata for all AI training data.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Adopt a digital commons approach to data and promote collaborative data sharing
controls.control_text_full: Member States, as also suggested in the report of the United Nations Secretary-General’s High-level Panel on Digital Cooperation, with the support of the United Nations and UNESCO, should adopt a digital commons approach to data where appropriate, increase interoperability of tools and datasets and interfaces of systems hosting data, and encourage private sector companies to share the data they collect with all stakeholders, as appropriate, for research, innovation or public benefits. They should also promote public and private efforts to create collaborative platforms to share quality data in trusted and secured data spaces.
controls.intent_summary: Governments should promote data as a shared public resource through digital commons, interoperable systems, and trusted platforms that enable secure, ethical data sharing between public and private actors for societal benefit.
evidence_signals: National digital commons policy framework for data sharing
Interoperable data interfaces across public and private systems
Trusted data spaces with governance and access controls
Public-private partnerships for shared AI datasets
Incentives for companies to contribute non-sensitive data to commons
evidence_anti_signals: Data silos maintained by private companies without sharing
No interoperability standards for data systems
No public-private data collaboration initiatives
No governance model for shared data spaces
controls.remediation_template: Establish a national digital commons strategy with interoperable standards and trusted data-sharing platforms to enable secure, ethical collaboration between public and private sectors for AI innovation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Include AI ethics in international and multi-stakeholder fora
controls.control_text_full: Member States and transnational corporations should prioritize AI ethics by including discussions of AI-related ethical issues into relevant international, intergovernmental and multi-stakeholder fora.
controls.intent_summary: Governments and corporations must integrate AI ethics into all major international policy discussions and multi-stakeholder forums to ensure global alignment on ethical AI norms.
evidence_signals: AI ethics agenda items in UN, OECD, or WTO meetings
Corporate participation in global AI ethics coalitions
Transnational corporate ethics statements referenced in international forums
Minutes or reports showing AI ethics as a standing agenda item
Joint declarations on AI ethics signed by multiple states and corporations
evidence_anti_signals: No mention of AI ethics in international trade or tech summits
Corporations absent from global AI governance dialogues
Policy documents ignoring ethical dimensions of AI
No cross-border coordination on AI ethics standards
controls.remediation_template: Integrate AI ethics as a standing agenda item in all relevant international and intergovernmental forums and require transnational corporations to participate in ethical AI dialogues.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure AI in development sectors adheres to ethical principles
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that the use of AI in areas of development such as education, science, culture, communication and information, health care, agriculture and food supply, environment, natural resource and infrastructure management, economic planning and growth, among others, adheres to the values and principles set forth in this Recommendation.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must ensure that all AI applications in critical development sectors—like health, education, and agriculture—comply with the ethical principles of the UNESCO Recommendation, including human rights and fairness.
evidence_signals: Ethical impact assessments for AI in public health or education
Procurement guidelines requiring ethical compliance in development AI
Public reports on AI use in agriculture or infrastructure with ethics review
Training programs for public officials on AI ethics in development
Audit reports linking AI deployments to UNESCO principles
evidence_anti_signals: AI deployed in schools or clinics without ethics review
No ethical criteria in public development project bids
AI systems in agriculture causing displacement without assessment
No alignment between AI projects and UNESCO’s ethical principles
controls.remediation_template: Require all AI systems deployed in development sectors to undergo ethical compliance reviews aligned with the UNESCO Recommendation, with public reporting and procurement enforcement.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Foster international cooperation on AI for development
controls.control_text_full: Member States should work through international organizations to provide platforms for international cooperation on AI for development, including by contributing expertise, funding, data, domain knowledge, infrastructure, and facilitating multi-stakeholder collaboration to tackle challenging development problems, especially for LMICs, in particular LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must actively support global cooperation to help low-income and vulnerable countries access AI expertise, funding, data, and infrastructure to address development challenges equitably.
evidence_signals: International AI development funds directed to LMICs
Capacity-building programs for researchers in LDCs/LLDCs/SIDS
Shared AI infrastructure projects in developing regions
Public reports on AI aid distribution to vulnerable nations
Partnerships between high-income and low-income research institutions
evidence_anti_signals: AI development aid concentrated in high-income countries
No funding or technical support for AI in LDCs
Data sharing restricted to wealthy nations
No inclusion of LMICs in global AI governance initiatives
