controls.remediation_template: Establish dedicated international funding and capacity-building programs to support AI development in low-income and vulnerable countries, ensuring equitable access to expertise, data, and infrastructure.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Promote international collaboration in AI research and innovation
controls.control_text_full: Member States should work to promote international collaboration on AI research and innovation, including research and innovation centres and networks that promote greater participation and leadership of researchers from LMICs and other countries, including LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must support global research networks that empower researchers from low-income and vulnerable countries to lead and participate in AI innovation, not just as data providers but as knowledge creators.
evidence_signals: Research centers led by LMIC researchers with international funding
Scholarships or fellowships for researchers from LDCs/LLDCs/SIDS
Joint publications with equitable authorship from Global South
International AI conferences with quotas for LMIC presenters
Funding programs requiring LMIC leadership in collaborative projects
evidence_anti_signals: AI research partnerships dominated by high-income country institutions
LMIC researchers excluded from leadership roles
No funding for research capacity in vulnerable regions
Global AI conferences with minimal representation from LMICs
controls.remediation_template: Create international AI research networks with mandatory leadership roles for researchers from low-income and vulnerable countries, supported by funding, training, and equitable publication practices.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Promote AI ethics research across cultures and contexts
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote AI ethics research by engaging international organizations and research institutions, as well as transnational corporations, that can be a basis for the ethical use of AI systems by public and private entities, including research into the applicability of specific ethical frameworks in specific cultures and contexts, and the possibilities to develop technologically feasible solutions in line with these frameworks.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must fund and support research that adapts AI ethics frameworks to local cultural, social, and technological contexts to ensure globally relevant and context-sensitive ethical AI.
evidence_signals: Research grants for culturally specific AI ethics studies
Publications on ethical AI in non-Western contexts
Collaborations between local communities and AI ethicists
Ethics frameworks adapted for indigenous knowledge systems
Case studies on AI ethics in religious or traditional societies
evidence_anti_signals: Only Western ethical frameworks applied globally
No research on cultural adaptation of AI ethics
AI ethics training materials ignore non-Western perspectives
No engagement with indigenous or local knowledge holders
controls.remediation_template: Fund and institutionalize research on culturally specific AI ethics, ensuring ethical frameworks are adapted and validated in diverse local contexts, especially in non-Western and indigenous communities.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Encourage international cooperation to bridge technological divides
controls.control_text_full: Member States should encourage international cooperation and collaboration in the field of AI to bridge geo-technological lines. Technological exchanges and consultations should take place between Member States and their populations, between the public and private sectors, and between and among the most and least technologically advanced countries in full respect of international law.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must foster equitable technological exchange between advanced and developing nations to reduce global AI disparities, ensuring cooperation respects international law and includes public participation.
evidence_signals: Technology transfer agreements between high- and low-income countries
Public consultations on AI deployment across borders
Joint public-private AI initiatives between developed and developing nations
International AI capacity-building programs with community input
Legal frameworks governing cross-border AI collaboration
evidence_anti_signals: AI technology exports without local consultation or capacity building
No mechanisms for low-tech countries to influence AI standards
One-way technology transfer without reciprocity
No public participation in cross-border AI dialogues
controls.remediation_template: Establish formal international cooperation mechanisms for equitable AI technology exchange, including public consultation, capacity building, and legal safeguards to bridge technological gaps between nations.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Assess and reduce environmental impact of AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States and business enterprises should assess the direct and indirect environmental impact throughout the AI system life cycle, including, but not limited to, its carbon footprint, energy consumption and the environmental impact of raw material extraction for supporting the manufacturing of AI technologies, and reduce the environmental impact of AI systems and data infrastructures. Member States should ensure compliance of all AI actors with environmental law, policies and practices.
controls.intent_summary: Governments and corporations must measure and minimize the environmental footprint of AI systems—including energy use, carbon emissions, and resource extraction—throughout their lifecycle and ensure compliance with environmental regulations.
evidence_signals: Carbon footprint reports for AI training runs
Energy efficiency standards for data centers hosting AI
Environmental impact assessments for AI projects
Procurement policies requiring low-carbon AI infrastructure
Compliance audits of AI actors against environmental laws
evidence_anti_signals: No tracking of energy use or emissions from AI systems
AI models trained on fossil-fuel-powered grids without offsetting
No environmental review in AI procurement or deployment
Raw material sourcing for AI hardware unmonitored for ecological harm
controls.remediation_template: Require all AI systems to undergo environmental impact assessments, report carbon and energy metrics, and comply with national and international environmental laws throughout their lifecycle.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Incentivize ethical AI for environmental resilience and sustainability
controls.control_text_full: Member States should introduce incentives, when needed and appropriate, to ensure the development and adoption of rights-based and ethical AI-powered solutions for disaster risk resilience; the monitoring, protection and regeneration of the environment and ecosystems; and the preservation of the planet. These AI systems should involve the participation of local and indigenous communities throughout the life cycle of AI systems and should support circular economy type approaches and sustainable consumption and production patterns. Some examples include using AI systems, when needed and appropriate, to: (a) Support the protection, monitoring and management of natural resources.
controls.intent_summary: Governments should incentivize the development of AI systems that protect the environment, support disaster resilience, and involve local and indigenous communities—aligned with circular economy and sustainable development principles.
evidence_signals: Grants or tax credits for AI systems supporting biodiversity or climate resilience
Co-design processes with indigenous communities for environmental AI
AI projects certified for circular economy alignment
Publicly funded AI for monitoring deforestation or water resources
Procurement preferences for ethical, community-involved environmental AI
evidence_anti_signals: Environmental AI deployed without community consultation
No incentives for sustainable AI development
AI systems contributing to resource overconsumption
No linkage between AI projects and circular economy goals
controls.remediation_template: Create incentive programs for ethical AI solutions that protect ecosystems, involve local and indigenous communities in design and deployment, and align with circular economy and sustainability goals.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Favor energy-efficient AI methods and apply the precautionary principle
controls.control_text_full: When choosing AI methods, given the potential data-intensive or resource-intensive character of some of them and the respective impact on the environment, Member States should ensure that AI actors, in line with the principle of proportionality, favour data, energy and resource-efficient AI methods. Requirements should be developed to ensure that appropriate evidence is available to show that an AI application will have the intended effect, or that safeguards accompanying an AI application can support the justification for its use. If this cannot be done, the precautionary principle must be favoured, and in instances where there are disproportionate negative impacts on the environment, AI should not be used.
controls.intent_summary: AI developers must prioritize low-resource models and provide evidence that the environmental cost is justified by the benefit; if evidence is lacking or impacts are excessive, the system must not be deployed.
evidence_signals: Model efficiency metrics (e.g., FLOPs, energy per inference) published in model cards
Environmental impact statements accompanying AI deployment proposals
Documentation of comparative analysis between high- and low-resource AI alternatives
Decisions to reject AI proposals due to disproportionate environmental cost
Regulatory requirements mandating energy-efficiency thresholds for public AI procurement
evidence_anti_signals: Use of large, unoptimized models without justification
No documentation of environmental trade-offs in AI selection
Deployment of AI systems with known high emissions despite available alternatives
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must conduct a proportionality assessment of its AI methods, document environmental trade-offs, and halt deployment of any system that cannot justify its environmental cost or violates the precautionary principle.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Maximize AI’s contribution to gender equality and prevent rights violations
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that the potential for digital technologies and artificial intelligence to contribute to achieving gender equality is fully maximized, and must ensure that the human rights and fundamental freedoms of girls and women, and their safety and integrity are not violated at any stage of the AI system life cycle. Moreover, Ethical Impact Assessment should include a transversal gender perspective.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed and deployed to actively advance gender equality and must never harm women or girls; all ethical assessments must explicitly integrate gender analysis across all phases of the AI lifecycle.
evidence_signals: Gender impact assessments integrated into AI ethical reviews
Data disaggregated by gender in training datasets and evaluation metrics
Policies mandating gender-sensitive design in public AI procurement
Reports documenting incidents of gender bias in AI systems and remediation steps
Inclusion of gender experts in AI development teams
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems trained on gender-neutral datasets that ignore systemic disparities
Ethical assessments that omit gender analysis entirely
No female representation in AI design or testing teams
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must integrate mandatory gender impact assessments into all AI development workflows and ensure that gender-based harm risks are mitigated before deployment.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Fund and implement gender-responsive digital policies and STEM programs
controls.control_text_full: Member States should have dedicated funds from their public budgets linked to financing gender-responsive schemes, ensure that national digital policies include a gender action plan, and develop relevant policies, for example, on labour education, targeted at supporting girls and women to make sure they are not left out of the digital economy powered by AI. Special investment in providing targeted programmes and gender-specific language, to increase the opportunities of girls’ and women’s participation in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM), including information and communication technologies (ICT) disciplines, preparedness, employability, equal career development and professional growth of girls and women, should be considered and implemented.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must allocate specific funding and design policies to ensure women and girls have equitable access to AI-related education, employment, and career advancement opportunities, particularly in STEM fields.
evidence_signals: Budget line items explicitly allocated for gender-responsive AI education
National digital strategy documents containing a gender action plan
Scholarships or mentorship programs for women in AI/STEM
Public reports on female enrollment and retention in ICT programs
Gender-targeted upskilling initiatives for women in the digital workforce
evidence_anti_signals: Absence of gender-specific funding in digital economy budgets
Digital policies that mention gender but lack concrete actions or metrics
No tracking of gender outcomes in AI workforce development programs
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government must establish a dedicated gender-responsive budget line and publish a measurable action plan to increase women’s participation in AI and digital economy pathways.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Eliminate gender gaps exacerbated by AI systems
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that the potential of AI systems to advance the achievement of gender equality is realized. They should ensure that these technologies do not exacerbate the already wide gender gaps existing in several fields in the analogue world, and instead eliminate those gaps. These gaps include: the gender wage gap; the unequal representation in certain professions and activities; the lack of representation at top management positions, boards of directors, or research teams in the AI field; the education gap; the digital and AI access, adoption, usage and affordability gap; and the unequal distribution of unpaid work and of the caring responsibilities in our societies.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must not reinforce existing gender inequalities in wages, representation, education, or care work; governments must actively use AI to close these gaps rather than deepen them.
evidence_signals: Gender-disaggregated data on AI workforce representation
Public reports on AI’s impact on wage gaps or unpaid labor distribution
Policies requiring gender-balanced representation on AI research teams
Surveys measuring AI access and affordability by gender
Interventions to reduce gender bias in algorithmic hiring or promotion tools
evidence_anti_signals: AI tools used in hiring that show gender bias in outcomes
No monitoring of AI’s impact on unpaid care work distribution
All-male or predominantly male AI leadership teams without diversity goals
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must audit its AI systems for gender-biased outcomes and implement corrective measures to actively reduce, not reinforce, gender disparities in employment, access, and social roles.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Prevent and redress gender stereotyping and online harm in AI
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that gender stereotyping and discriminatory biases are not translated into AI systems, and instead identify and proactively redress these. Efforts are necessary to avoid the compounding negative effect of technological divides in achieving gender equality and avoiding violence such as harassment, bullying or trafficking of girls and women and under-represented groups, including in the online domain.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed to avoid reinforcing harmful gender stereotypes and must include safeguards against online gender-based violence, harassment, and trafficking enabled or amplified by algorithmic systems.
evidence_signals: Bias audits of NLP models for gendered language patterns
Reporting mechanisms for AI-enabled online harassment
Training datasets filtered for gender stereotypes
Collaborations with NGOs to identify AI-facilitated trafficking risks
Policies banning AI tools that reinforce gendered roles (e.g., caregiving bots)
evidence_anti_signals: AI chatbots or voice assistants using stereotypical female voices for service roles
No mechanisms to report AI-enabled gender-based harassment
Training data containing gendered stereotypes without mitigation
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must conduct a gender bias audit of its AI systems, remove stereotypical content from training data, and implement reporting and response protocols for AI-facilitated gender-based harm.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote female entrepreneurship and gender-balanced leadership in AI
controls.control_text_full: Member States should encourage female entrepreneurship, participation and engagement in all stages of an AI system life cycle by offering and promoting economic, regulatory incentives, among other incentives and support schemes, as well as policies that aim at a balanced gender participation in AI research in academia, gender representation on digital and AI companies’ top management positions, boards of directors and research teams. Member States should ensure that public funds (for innovation, research and technologies) are channelled to inclusive programmes and companies, with clear gender representation, and that private funds are similarly encouraged through affirmative action principles. Policies on harassment-free environments should be developed and enforced, together with the encouragement of the transfer of best practices on how to promote diversity throughout the AI system life cycle.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must actively promote women’s leadership and entrepreneurship in AI through funding, policy, and workplace protections, ensuring gender-balanced representation in research, management, and innovation.
evidence_signals: Public funding criteria requiring gender-balanced teams for grants
Publicly reported gender diversity metrics for AI company leadership
Harassment-free workplace policies specific to AI/tech sectors
Incubators or accelerators focused on women-led AI startups
Best practice guides on gender diversity in AI teams published by government
evidence_anti_signals: AI funding programs with no gender representation requirements
All-male executive teams in publicly funded AI projects
Absence of policies addressing harassment in AI research environments
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government must revise funding criteria to require gender-balanced teams and enforce workplace policies that prevent harassment and promote women’s leadership in AI.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Promote gender diversity in AI research and combat harassment
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote gender diversity in AI research in academia and industry by offering incentives to girls and women to enter the field, putting in place mechanisms to fight gender stereotyping and harassment within the AI research community, and encouraging academic and private entities to share best practices on how to enhance gender diversity.
controls.intent_summary: Academic and industry institutions must actively recruit and retain women in AI research by removing barriers, combating harassment, and sharing proven strategies for inclusive research environments.
evidence_signals: Scholarships or fellowships for women in AI research
Anonymous reporting systems for harassment in AI labs
Publicly shared diversity action plans from universities and tech firms
Mentorship programs pairing female students with AI researchers
Conference policies requiring gender-balanced speaker lineups
evidence_anti_signals: No diversity goals or reporting in AI research departments
Harassment complaints in AI labs with no investigation or resolution
Conferences or publications with all-male authorship or speaker panels
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, institutions must implement targeted recruitment, anti-harassment protocols, and public diversity reporting for AI research programs.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Use AI to preserve and promote cultural heritage, including indigenous languages
controls.control_text_full: Member States are encouraged to incorporate AI systems, where appropriate, in the preservation, enrichment, understanding, promotion, management and accessibility of tangible, documentary and intangible cultural heritage, including endangered languages as well as indigenous languages and knowledges, for example by introducing or updating educational programmes related to the application of AI systems in these areas, where appropriate, and by ensuring a participatory approach, targeted at institutions and the public.
controls.intent_summary: AI should be used to protect and revitalize cultural heritage and indigenous languages, with active involvement of affected communities in design and deployment to ensure cultural integrity.
evidence_signals: AI tools developed in partnership with indigenous communities to document languages
Publicly accessible digital archives of endangered languages powered by AI
Educational curricula integrating AI for cultural heritage preservation
Community consent documentation for AI use of cultural data
Projects using AI to restore or translate historical texts in native languages
evidence_anti_signals: AI tools trained on cultural data without community consultation
Digital archives of heritage materials without metadata on provenance or consent
AI translation tools that erase tonal or dialectal nuances of indigenous languages
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must engage cultural communities in co-designing AI tools for heritage preservation and obtain free, prior, and informed consent for all cultural data use.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Assess cultural impact of NLP systems on language diversity
controls.control_text_full: Member States should examine and address the cultural impact of AI systems, especially natural language processing (NLP) applications such as automated translation and voice assistants, on the nuances of human language and expression. Such assessments should provide input for the design and implementation of strategies that maximize the benefits from these systems by bridging cultural gaps and increasing human understanding, as well as addressing the negative implications such as the reduction of use, which could lead to the disappearance of endangered languages, local dialects, and tonal and cultural variations associated with human language and expression.
controls.intent_summary: NLP systems must be evaluated for their impact on linguistic diversity; policies must prevent AI from accelerating the extinction of endangered languages and dialects by favoring dominant languages.
evidence_signals: Cultural impact assessments of NLP models on minority languages
Language coverage metrics for AI translation tools (including low-resource languages)
Funding allocated to develop NLP for endangered languages
Public reports on AI’s role in language shift or erosion
Collaborations with linguists to audit AI for cultural bias
evidence_anti_signals: AI translation tools that only support major global languages
No documentation of linguistic diversity impact in NLP model cards
Voice assistants that cannot recognize or respond to regional dialects
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must assess its NLP systems for linguistic bias and invest in developing or supporting AI tools for under-resourced and endangered languages.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Train artists and creatives in AI for cultural production
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote AI education and digital training for artists and creative professionals to assess the suitability of AI technologies for use in their profession, and contribute to the design and implementation of suitable AI technologies, as AI technologies are being used to create, produce, distribute, broadcast and consume a variety of cultural goods and services, bearing in mind the importance of preserving cultural heritage, diversity and artistic freedom.
controls.intent_summary: Artists and cultural creators must be equipped with AI literacy to use, critique, and co-design AI tools in cultural production, ensuring that artistic freedom and cultural diversity are preserved.
evidence_signals: AI training programs for artists offered by cultural institutions
Collaborative projects between AI developers and artists
Publications or exhibitions on AI and artistic expression
Policies protecting artists’ rights in AI-generated content
Funding for AI-augmented cultural projects led by creatives
evidence_anti_signals: AI tools for art creation developed without artist input
No training offered to cultural professionals on AI use
Artists excluded from AI design teams in cultural institutions
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, cultural institutions must provide AI literacy training for artists and ensure their meaningful participation in the design of AI tools used in cultural creation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure algorithmic recommendation promotes diverse cultural content
controls.control_text_full: Member States should engage technology companies and other stakeholders to promote a diverse supply of and plural access to cultural expressions, and in particular to ensure that algorithmic recommendation enhances the visibility and discoverability of local content.
controls.intent_summary: Algorithmic recommendation systems must be designed to promote local and diverse cultural content, not just dominant or commercial content, to prevent cultural homogenization.
evidence_signals: Transparency reports from platforms on local content recommendation rates
Algorithmic audits showing equitable visibility for regional content
Public-private partnerships to boost local cultural content online
Policy mandates requiring diversity thresholds in recommendation engines
User tools allowing customization of cultural content preferences
evidence_anti_signals: Recommendation systems that suppress local or minority cultural content
No metrics reported on cultural diversity in algorithmic outputs
Platforms prioritizing global hits over regional or indigenous content
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, platforms must audit their recommendation algorithms for cultural bias and implement measures to ensure equitable visibility for local and minority cultural expressions.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Assess AI’s impact on intellectual property rights
controls.control_text_full: Member States should foster new research at the intersection between AI and intellectual property (IP), for example to determine whether or how to protect with IP rights the works created by means of AI technologies. Member States should also assess how AI technologies are affecting the rights or interests of IP owners, whose works are used to research, develop, train or implement AI applications.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must study and clarify how AI-generated works affect copyright and IP law, and ensure that training AI on protected works respects the rights of original creators.
evidence_signals: Government-commissioned reports on AI and IP law reform
Legal frameworks distinguishing AI-generated vs. human-authored works
Licensing agreements for training data that compensate rights holders
Transparency in training data sources for AI models
Court rulings or policy guidelines on AI-generated content ownership
evidence_anti_signals: AI models trained on copyrighted works without permission or attribution
No legal clarity on ownership of AI-generated art, music, or text
Platforms claiming ownership of AI outputs derived from protected works
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must conduct an IP impact assessment of its AI training data and ensure compliance with existing copyright frameworks or advocate for legal clarity.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Provide AI literacy education to the public at all levels
controls.control_text_full: Member States should work with international organizations, educational institutions and private and non-governmental entities to provide adequate AI literacy education to the public on all levels in all countries in order to empower people and reduce the digital divides and digital access inequalities resulting from the wide adoption of AI systems.
controls.intent_summary: All citizens, regardless of background, must receive accessible AI literacy education to understand AI’s benefits, risks, and societal impacts, reducing digital exclusion.
evidence_signals: National AI literacy curricula for schools and adult learners
Public awareness campaigns on AI and digital rights
Free online AI education modules in multiple languages
Partnerships with NGOs to deliver AI literacy in underserved regions
Surveys measuring public understanding of AI before and after education programs
evidence_anti_signals: No public AI education initiatives beyond technical professionals
AI literacy materials available only in dominant languages
Digital exclusion persists despite AI adoption
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government must launch a nationwide AI literacy program accessible to all demographics, with materials in local languages and tailored to non-technical audiences.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Promote prerequisite skills for AI education
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote the acquisition of ‘prerequisite skills’ for AI education, such as basic literacy, numeracy, coding and digital skills, and media and information literacy, as well as critical and creative thinking, teamwork, communication, socio-emotional and AI ethics skills, especially in countries and in regions or areas within countries where there are notable gaps in the education of these skills.
controls.intent_summary: Foundational skills—including digital, ethical, and socio-emotional competencies—must be prioritized in education systems to prepare populations for an AI-driven world, especially in underserved areas.
evidence_signals: National education standards incorporating AI ethics and digital literacy
Curriculum updates including critical thinking and AI ethics in primary/secondary schools
Targeted programs to close skill gaps in rural or marginalized regions
Teacher training modules on AI ethics and digital skills
Assessments measuring student proficiency in prerequisite AI skills
evidence_anti_signals: AI education initiatives that skip foundational skills
No integration of ethics or critical thinking into digital curricula
Skill gaps persisting in regions with high AI adoption
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the education system must revise curricula to embed AI-relevant foundational skills—especially ethics, digital literacy, and critical thinking—in all levels of schooling, with targeted support for underserved regions.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Raise public awareness of AI’s impact on human rights
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote general awareness programmes about AI developments, including on data and the opportunities and challenges brought about by AI technologies, the impact of AI systems on human rights and their implications, including children’s rights. These programmes should be accessible to non-technical as well as technical groups.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must run public awareness campaigns that explain how AI affects human rights—including children’s rights—so that all citizens, not just experts, can understand and engage with these technologies responsibly.
evidence_signals: Public service announcements on AI and human rights
Multilingual educational materials on AI risks and benefits
School programs teaching children about AI and privacy
Community workshops on AI and rights in local languages
Government websites with clear, non-technical AI explainers
evidence_anti_signals: No public outreach on AI’s human rights implications
Awareness materials only in technical jargon
Children’s rights not addressed in AI education campaigns
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government must launch accessible, rights-based public awareness campaigns on AI’s societal impacts, with special attention to children and non-technical audiences.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure ethical and supportive use of AI in education
controls.control_text_full: Member States should encourage research initiatives on the responsible and ethical use of AI technologies in teaching, teacher training and e-learning, among other issues, to enhance opportunities and mitigate the challenges and risks involved in this area. The initiatives should be accompanied by an adequate assessment of the quality of education and impact on students and teachers of the use of AI technologies. Member States should also ensure that AI technologies empower students and teachers and enhance their experience, bearing in mind that relational and social aspects and the value of traditional forms of education are vital in teacher-student and student-student relationships and should be considered when discussing the adoption of AI technologies in education. AI systems used in learning should be subject to strict requirements when it comes to the monitoring, assessment of abilities, or prediction of the learners’ behaviours. AI should support the learning process without reducing cognitive abilities and without extracting sensitive information, in compliance with relevant personal data protection standards. The data handed
controls.intent_summary: AI in education must enhance learning without replacing human interaction, must not predict or monitor students in invasive ways, and must comply with data protection laws to safeguard privacy and cognitive development.
evidence_signals: Ethical guidelines for AI in classrooms prohibiting behavioral prediction
Data protection impact assessments for educational AI tools
Research studies on AI’s impact on student cognition and social skills
Policies banning AI surveillance in schools without consent
Teacher training on using AI as a supplement, not a replacement, for pedagogy
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems that track or predict student behavior without consent
Schools using AI for high-stakes grading without human oversight
Educational AI tools collecting sensitive data without privacy safeguards
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, educational institutions must immediately halt any AI system that monitors or predicts student behavior without consent, and implement strict data protection and human-centered pedagogical guidelines for all AI tools.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Promote inclusive participation in AI education
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote the participation and leadership of girls and women, diverse ethnicities and cultures, persons with disabilities, marginalized and vulnerable people or people in vulnerable situations, minorities and all persons not enjoying the full benefits of digital inclusion, in AI education programmes at all levels, as well as the monitoring and sharing of best practices in this regard with other Member States.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must actively ensure equitable access and leadership opportunities in AI education for underrepresented and marginalized groups, and track progress through shared best practices.
evidence_signals: Demographic data showing participation rates of marginalized groups in AI programs
Publicly reported best practices on inclusive AI education
Scholarships or targeted outreach programs for underrepresented communities
Inclusive curriculum design reviewed by diverse stakeholders
Partnerships with community organizations serving vulnerable populations
evidence_anti_signals: AI education programs with no diversity metrics or reporting
Absence of outreach to marginalized communities
Curricula that assume universal digital access or literacy
No representation of diverse cultural perspectives in AI content
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the institution should develop and publish a diversity and inclusion action plan for AI education, with measurable targets and partnerships with community organizations representing underrepresented groups.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Develop AI ethics curricula in local and indigenous languages
controls.control_text_full: Member States should develop, in accordance with their national education programmes and traditions, AI ethics curricula for all levels, and promote cross-collaboration between AI technical skills education and humanistic, ethical and social aspects of AI education. Online courses and digital resources of AI ethics education should be developed in local languages, including indigenous languages, and take into account the diversity of environments, especially ensuring accessibility of formats for persons with disabilities.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must create AI ethics education materials in local and indigenous languages, ensure accessibility for persons with disabilities, and integrate ethical and social dimensions with technical training.
evidence_signals: AI ethics courses available in indigenous and local languages
Accessible formats (e.g., screen-reader compatible, sign language) for digital resources
Curriculum documents showing integration of ethics with technical AI modules
Evidence of collaboration between humanities and technical departments
Publicly listed language options for AI education platforms
evidence_anti_signals: AI ethics materials only in dominant global languages (e.g., English)
Digital resources lacking accessibility features
Ethics content siloed separately from technical training
No mention of indigenous knowledge systems in curricula
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the education authority should prioritize translating AI ethics materials into local and indigenous languages, ensure compliance with accessibility standards, and integrate ethics modules into all technical AI courses.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote and fund AI ethics research and ethical AI best practices
controls.control_text_full: Member States should promote and support AI research, notably AI ethics research, including for example through investing in such research or by creating incentives for the public and private sectors to invest in this area, recognizing that research contributes significantly to the further development and improvement of AI technologies with a view to promoting international law and the values and principles set forth in this Recommendation. Member States should also publicly promote the best practices of, and cooperation with, researchers and companies who develop AI in an ethical manner.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must fund and incentivize AI ethics research and publicly recognize ethical AI developers to encourage responsible innovation aligned with international norms.
evidence_signals: Government grants or tax incentives for AI ethics research
Public lists of recognized ethical AI developers or projects
Annual reports on funding allocated to AI ethics research
Conferences or awards for ethical AI innovation
Public statements by officials endorsing ethical AI practices
evidence_anti_signals: No public funding allocated to AI ethics research
Absence of recognition programs for ethical AI developers
Funding skewed exclusively toward technical performance metrics
No public documentation of ethical AI best practices
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should establish a dedicated funding stream for AI ethics research and launch a public recognition program for organizations demonstrating ethical AI development.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Require AI researchers to integrate research ethics into their work
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that AI researchers are trained in research ethics and require them to include ethical considerations in their designs, products and publications, especially in the analyses of the datasets they use, how they are annotated, and the quality and scope of the results with possible applications.
controls.intent_summary: AI researchers must receive formal ethics training and be required to document ethical considerations in dataset selection, annotation practices, and result interpretation to prevent biased or harmful outcomes.
evidence_signals: Mandatory ethics training certificates for AI researchers
Ethics review sections in research publications or project proposals
Documentation of dataset provenance and annotation guidelines
Institutional review boards evaluating AI research for ethical risks
Peer-reviewed papers explicitly addressing bias or limitations in data
evidence_anti_signals: Research papers with no mention of dataset biases or ethical constraints
Absence of ethics training in AI graduate programs
Undocumented or proprietary annotation processes
Claims of 'neutral' or 'objective' AI without ethical analysis
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, research institutions should mandate ethics training for all AI researchers and require ethical impact statements in all publications and project proposals involving AI systems.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Facilitate data access for research in low-resource countries
controls.control_text_full: Member States should encourage private sector companies to facilitate the access of the scientific community to their data for research, especially in LMICs, in particular LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS. This access should conform to relevant privacy and data protection standards.
controls.intent_summary: Private companies must enable researchers in low-resource countries to access their data for ethical AI research, while ensuring compliance with privacy and data protection laws.
evidence_signals: Public data access agreements with LMIC research institutions
Anonymized datasets shared via secure platforms for global researchers
Documentation of data use agreements respecting local privacy laws
Partnerships between tech firms and universities in SIDS/LDCs
Transparency reports detailing data sharing practices
evidence_anti_signals: Data access restricted to high-income country institutions only
No data sharing policies or opaque data governance
Use of data without consent or anonymization
Lack of support for local researchers to request or use data
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, companies should establish a transparent data access program for researchers in low-income countries, with clear privacy safeguards and support for local capacity building.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure rigorous, interdisciplinary AI research for critical evaluation
controls.control_text_full: To ensure a critical evaluation of AI research and proper monitoring of potential misuses or adverse effects, Member States should ensure that any future developments with regards to AI technologies should be based on rigorous and independent scientific research, and promote interdisciplinary AI research by including disciplines other than science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM), such as cultural studies, education, ethics, international relations, law, linguistics, philosophy, political science, sociology and psychology.
controls.intent_summary: AI development must be grounded in independent, interdisciplinary research that includes social sciences and humanities to identify and mitigate societal risks and misuses.
evidence_signals: Research projects funded across STEM and non-STEM disciplines
Interdisciplinary review boards for AI projects
Publications co-authored by ethicists, sociologists, and engineers
Funding calls explicitly requiring non-STEM collaborators
Reports analyzing AI impacts through legal, cultural, or psychological lenses
evidence_anti_signals: AI research teams composed solely of engineers or computer scientists
No involvement of social science experts in AI design or evaluation
Project proposals that dismiss ethical or societal concerns as 'non-technical'
Funding restricted exclusively to technical performance metrics
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, funding agencies and institutions should require interdisciplinary teams including ethicists, sociologists, and legal experts in all major AI research initiatives and evaluate proposals based on societal impact analysis.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure scientific rigor in data-driven AI conclusions
controls.control_text_full: Recognizing that AI technologies present great opportunities to help advance scientific knowledge and practice, especially in traditionally model-driven disciplines, Member States should encourage scientific communities to be aware of the benefits, limits and risks of their use; this includes attempting to ensure that conclusions drawn from data-driven approaches, models and treatments are robust and sound. Furthermore, Member States should welcome and support the role of the scientific community in contributing to policy and in cultivating awareness of the strengths and weaknesses of AI technologies.
controls.intent_summary: Scientific communities must critically assess the validity and limitations of AI-driven conclusions and actively inform policy with transparent evaluations of AI’s strengths and risks.
evidence_signals: Peer-reviewed papers explicitly discussing limitations of AI models
Scientific societies issuing guidelines on AI use in research
Policy briefs authored by scientists on AI risks and benefits
Workshops training scientists in AI literacy and critical evaluation
Institutional policies requiring AI model validation before publication
evidence_anti_signals: Scientific publications presenting AI results as definitive without uncertainty analysis
No institutional guidance on AI use in research
Scientists lacking training in evaluating algorithmic bias or data artifacts
Policy decisions made without input from scientific experts on AI
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, scientific institutions should implement mandatory training on AI limitations and require all AI-based research to include explicit discussion of uncertainty, bias, and reproducibility in publications.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Use AI to improve access to information and knowledge
controls.control_text_full: Member States should use AI systems to improve access to information and knowledge. This can include support to researchers, academia, journalists, the general public and developers, to enhance freedom of expression, academic and scientific freedoms, access to information, and increased proactive disclosure of official data and information.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must deploy AI to expand equitable access to information and knowledge, supporting freedom of expression and transparency in public data disclosure.
evidence_signals: AI-powered public portals for open government data
Tools enabling journalists to analyze public datasets
AI systems supporting multilingual access to educational resources
Publicly documented policies on AI use for information access
Metrics tracking improvements in information accessibility
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems used only to restrict or filter information
No public AI tools for accessing government or academic data
Information access tools available only in dominant languages
Lack of transparency about how AI curates or prioritizes information
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should launch AI-enabled public information platforms with multilingual support and ensure proactive disclosure of official data with clear documentation of AI usage.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure transparency and redress in AI content moderation
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that AI actors respect and promote freedom of expression as well as access to information with regard to automated content generation, moderation and curation. Appropriate frameworks, including regulation, should enable transparency of online communication and information operators and ensure users have access to a diversity of viewpoints, as well as processes for prompt notification to the users on the reasons for removal or other treatment of content, and appeal mechanisms that allow users to seek redress.
controls.intent_summary: AI-driven content moderation must be transparent, provide clear reasons for content removal, offer appeal mechanisms, and preserve diversity of viewpoints to protect freedom of expression.
evidence_signals: Public transparency reports from platforms on content moderation decisions
User notifications explaining why content was removed or altered
Accessible appeal processes for contested content decisions
Regulations requiring algorithmic accountability in content curation
Audits showing diversity of viewpoints preserved in algorithmic feeds
evidence_anti_signals: No public explanations for content removals
No user appeal process for AI-mediated decisions
Algorithms suppressing minority or dissenting viewpoints
Opaque moderation criteria or proprietary black-box systems
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, platforms must implement transparent content moderation policies, provide users with clear reasons for removals, and establish independent appeal processes with human oversight.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote digital and media literacy to counter disinformation
controls.control_text_full: Member States should invest in and promote digital and media and information literacy skills to strengthen critical thinking and competencies needed to understand the use and implication of AI systems, in order to mitigate and counter disinformation, misinformation and hate speech. A better understanding and evaluation of both the positive and potentially harmful effects of recommender systems should be part of those efforts.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must invest in public education to build digital literacy skills that enable people to critically assess AI-driven content, including recommender systems, and combat disinformation.
evidence_signals: National digital literacy curricula in schools and adult education
Public campaigns on identifying AI-generated disinformation
Training programs for teachers on AI and media literacy
Evaluation metrics for media literacy program effectiveness
Resources explaining how recommender systems influence content exposure
evidence_anti_signals: No public media literacy initiatives targeting AI risks
Curricula that omit AI and algorithmic influence
Lack of teacher training on digital misinformation
No public awareness campaigns on recommender system biases
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the education ministry should integrate AI and media literacy into national curricula and launch public awareness campaigns focused on algorithmic influence and misinformation detection.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Enable ethical media reporting on AI and ethical AI use in media
controls.control_text_full: Member States should create enabling environments for media to have the rights and resources to effectively report on the benefits and harms of AI systems, and also encourage media to make ethical use of AI systems in their operations.
controls.intent_summary: Media organizations must be supported with resources and legal protections to report critically on AI, while also being encouraged to use AI ethically in their own content production.
evidence_signals: Funding or grants for investigative journalism on AI
Media guidelines on ethical AI use (e.g., deepfakes, automated reporting)
Training programs for journalists on AI literacy
Publicly available media audits on AI use in newsrooms
Legal protections for journalists reporting on AI harms
evidence_anti_signals: Media outlets using AI-generated content without disclosure
No funding or training for AI-related journalism
Censorship or legal barriers to reporting on AI risks
Newsrooms using AI for content generation without ethical review
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should establish funding and legal protections for AI-focused journalism and issue ethical guidelines for media organizations using AI in content creation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Address AI’s impact on labor markets and update education skills
controls.control_text_full: Member States should assess and address the impact of AI systems on labour markets and its implications for education requirements, in all countries and with special emphasis on countries where the economy is labour-intensive. This can include the introduction of a wider range of ‘core’ and interdisciplinary skills at all education levels to provide current workers and new generations a fair chance of finding jobs in a rapidly changing market, and to ensure their awareness of the ethical aspects of AI systems. Skills such as ‘learning how to learn’, communication, critical thinking, teamwork, empathy, and the ability to transfer one’s knowledge across domains, should be taught alongside specialist, technical skills, as well as low-skilled tasks. Being transparent about what skills are in demand and updating curricula around these are key.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must update education curricula to include essential human and interdisciplinary skills alongside technical training, and ensure transparency about evolving labor demands in the age of AI.
evidence_signals: Updated national education standards incorporating AI-era skills
Public dashboards showing in-demand skills by sector
Curricula that include empathy, critical thinking, and adaptability
Partnerships between schools and employers to align training
Ethics modules integrated into vocational and technical training
evidence_anti_signals: Curricula unchanged since before AI proliferation
No public data on skills in demand or job displacement risks
Technical training without any human or ethical components
Vocational programs ignoring AI’s impact on job roles
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the education authority should revise national curricula to embed AI-era soft and interdisciplinary skills, publish real-time labor demand data, and integrate ethics training into all vocational programs.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Foster collaboration to align training with future work needs
controls.control_text_full: Member States should support collaboration agreements among governments, academic institutions, vocational education and training institutions, industry, workers’ organizations and civil society to bridge the gap of skillset requirements to align training programmes and strategies with the implications of the future of work and the needs of industry, including small and medium enterprises. Project-based teaching and learning approaches for AI should be promoted, allowing for partnerships between public institutions, private sector companies, universities and research centres.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must facilitate multi-stakeholder collaboration to align education and training programs with evolving AI-driven labor needs, using project-based learning models.
evidence_signals: Formal collaboration agreements between industry and educational institutions
Project-based AI training programs with industry partners
Joint curriculum development committees with employers and unions
Publicly listed partnerships between universities and SMEs
Internship or apprenticeship programs tied to AI skill development
evidence_anti_signals: No industry input in curriculum design
Training programs isolated from labor market needs
Absence of partnerships with small businesses
No project-based or experiential learning components
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should mandate industry-education partnerships and fund project-based AI training initiatives that involve SMEs, unions, and academic institutions.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure fair transition for workers displaced by AI
controls.control_text_full: Member States should work with private sector companies, civil society organizations and other stakeholders, including workers and unions to ensure a fair transition for at-risk employees. This includes putting in place upskilling and reskilling programmes, finding effective mechanisms of retaining employees during those transition periods, and exploring ‘safety net’ programmes for those who cannot be retrained. Member States should develop and implement programmes to research and address the challenges identified that could include upskilling and reskilling, enhanced social protection, proactive industry policies and interventions, tax benefits, new taxation forms, among others. Member States should ensure that there is sufficient public funding to support these programmes. Relevant regulations, such as tax regimes, should be carefully examined and changed if needed to counteract the consequences of unemployment caused by AI-based automation.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must implement comprehensive worker transition programs—including upskilling, income support, and tax reforms—to mitigate job losses from AI automation, with adequate public funding and stakeholder collaboration.
evidence_signals: Government-funded reskilling programs for displaced workers
Social safety nets (e.g., unemployment extensions, wage insurance)
Tax policy changes incentivizing human employment over automation
Tripartite committees (government, employers, unions) on AI transition
Public reports on AI-induced job displacement and mitigation efforts
evidence_anti_signals: No reskilling programs for workers in automated sectors
No public funding allocated to worker transition
Tax policies favoring automation over human labor
Absence of worker or union representation in AI policy planning
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should establish a funded worker transition program with reskilling, income support, and tax incentives, and create a tripartite council including unions and employers to oversee implementation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Support interdisciplinary research on AI’s labor impact
controls.control_text_full: Member States should encourage and support researchers to analyse the impact of AI systems on the local labour environment in order to anticipate future trends and challenges. These studies should have an interdisciplinary approach and investigate the impact of AI systems on economic, social and geographic sectors, as well as on human-robot interactions and human-human relationships, in order to advise on reskilling and redeployment best practices.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must fund interdisciplinary research to understand how AI affects local labor markets, human interactions, and geographic disparities, to inform effective reskilling and redeployment strategies.
evidence_signals: Research grants for interdisciplinary labor-AI studies
Publicly available reports on regional AI labor impacts
Studies analyzing human-robot interaction in workplaces
Collaborations between economists, sociologists, and engineers
Policy recommendations based on localized labor impact data
evidence_anti_signals: No research on AI’s localized labor effects
Studies limited to technical efficiency metrics
Absence of social or geographic analysis in labor impact reports
No engagement with affected communities in research design
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should launch a competitive grant program for interdisciplinary research on AI’s labor impacts, requiring collaboration between social scientists, economists, and affected communities.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Prevent market dominance and ensure competitive AI markets
controls.control_text_full: Member States should take appropriate steps to ensure competitive markets and consumer protection, considering possible measures and mechanisms at national, regional and international levels, to prevent abuse of dominant market positions, including by monopolies, in relation to AI systems throughout their life cycle, whether these are data, research, technology, or market. Member States should prevent the resulting inequalities, assess relevant markets and promote competitive markets. Due consideration should be given to LMICs, in particular LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, which are more exposed and vulnerable to the possibility of abuses of market dominance as a result of a lack of infrastructure, human capacity and regulations, among other factors. AI actors developing AI systems in countries which have established or adopted ethical standards on AI should respect these standards when exporting these products, developing or applying their AI systems in countries where
controls.intent_summary: Member States must enforce antitrust measures and market oversight to prevent monopolistic control over AI data, technology, or markets, especially protecting vulnerable countries from exploitation, and require ethical compliance in cross-border AI exports.
evidence_signals: Antitrust investigations into AI monopolies
Regulations requiring interoperability or data portability in AI systems
Export controls requiring ethical compliance for AI products abroad
Market impact assessments focused on AI concentration
Support programs for local AI startups in LMICs to compete globally
evidence_anti_signals: No enforcement against AI market dominance
AI systems locked into proprietary ecosystems with no interoperability
Ethical standards ignored in exports to low-regulation countries
Absence of market analysis on AI concentration risks
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, regulators should initiate antitrust reviews of dominant AI firms, mandate interoperability standards, and require ethical compliance certification for AI exports to countries with weaker governance.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Respect ethical AI standards when exporting systems
controls.control_text_full: AI actors developing AI systems in countries which have established or adopted ethical standards on AI should respect these standards when exporting these products, developing or applying their AI systems in countries where such standards may not exist, while respecting applicable international law and domestic legislation, standards and practices of these countries.
controls.intent_summary: AI developers must apply the ethical standards of their home country even when deploying systems abroad, ensuring global consistency in ethical practices and preventing ethical arbitrage.
evidence_signals: Export compliance checklists requiring ethical standard adherence
Documentation showing ethical review of AI products before international deployment
Publicly disclosed ethical impact assessments for exported AI systems
Contracts with foreign partners requiring adherence to home-country AI ethics
Training programs for developers on cross-border ethical obligations
evidence_anti_signals: No ethical review for exported AI systems
Marketing materials that claim 'no ethics requirements abroad'
Lack of traceability in AI deployment locations
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, companies must implement a mandatory ethical compliance protocol for all exported AI systems, requiring alignment with the highest applicable ethical standards regardless of local regulations.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Deploy AI in health care consistent with human rights and international law
controls.control_text_full: Member States should endeavour to employ effective AI systems for improving human health and protecting the right to life, including mitigating disease outbreaks, while building and maintaining international solidarity to tackle global health risks and uncertainties, and ensure that their deployment of AI systems in health care be consistent with international law and their human rights law obligations.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must ensure all health-related AI systems uphold human rights and international legal obligations, prioritizing life-saving applications while avoiding rights violations such as discrimination or lack of consent.
evidence_signals: Human rights impact assessments for health AI deployments
Documentation of compliance with WHO or UN human rights guidelines
Publicly available legal reviews of health AI systems
Partnerships with international health bodies for AI deployment
Audit trails showing adherence to right-to-life principles
evidence_anti_signals: Health AI systems deployed without human rights review
Absence of legal compliance documentation
Use of AI in health without transparency about data sources
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, health authorities must require all AI systems used in healthcare to undergo mandatory human rights and international law compliance reviews before deployment.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Ensure health AI systems are safe, effective, and evidence-based
controls.control_text_full: Member States should ensure that the development and deployment of AI systems related to health in general and mental health in particular, paying due attention to children and youth, is regulated to the effect that they are safe, effective, efficient, scientifically and medically proven and enable evidence-based innovation and medical progress.
controls.intent_summary: All health AI systems, especially those targeting mental health and youth, must be rigorously validated through scientific and medical evidence before use, ensuring they are safe, effective, and not deployed prematurely.
evidence_signals: Clinical trial data supporting AI health tools
Regulatory approval from national health authorities
Peer-reviewed publications validating AI efficacy
Post-deployment outcome tracking for health AI
Certification standards for medical AI systems
evidence_anti_signals: AI tools marketed for health without clinical validation
No regulatory oversight for mental health chatbots
Use of unproven algorithms in public health programs
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, regulators must ban the deployment of any health AI system lacking peer-reviewed clinical validation and formal regulatory approval.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Involve patients in digital health intervention development
controls.control_text_full: Moreover, in the related area of digital health interventions, Member States are strongly encouraged to actively involve patients and their representatives in all relevant steps of the development of the system.
controls.intent_summary: Patients and their advocates must be meaningfully included throughout the design, testing, and deployment of digital health AI tools to ensure systems reflect real user needs and values.
evidence_signals: Patient advisory boards involved in AI health product design
Public records of patient consultation sessions
Co-design workshops documented in development logs
Patient feedback integrated into system iterations
Ethics committee reports citing patient input
evidence_anti_signals: No patient representation in development teams
Development documentation shows no user feedback loops
Health AI systems launched without user testing
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, health AI developers must establish mandatory patient engagement protocols at every stage of development, with documented participation and feedback integration.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Regulate health AI to minimize bias and ensure human oversight
controls.control_text_full: Member States should pay particular attention in regulating prediction, detection and treatment solutions for health care in AI applications by: (a) ensuring oversight to minimize and mitigate bias; (b) ensuring that the professional, the patient, caregiver or service user is included as a “domain expert” in the team in all relevant steps when developing the algorithms; (c) paying due attention to privacy because of the potential need for being medically monitored and ensuring that all relevant national and international data protection requirements are met; (d) ensuring effective mechanisms so that those whose personal data is being analysed are aware of and provide informed consent for the use and analysis of their data, without preventing access to health care; (e) ensuring the human care and final decision of diagnosis and treatment are taken always by humans while acknowledging that AI systems can also assist in their work; (f) ensuring, where necessary, the review of AI systems by an ethical research committee prior to clinical use.
controls.intent_summary: Health AI systems must be designed with bias mitigation, human-in-the-loop decision-making, informed consent, privacy compliance, and ethical review — ensuring AI supports but never replaces human clinical judgment.
evidence_signals: Bias audits conducted on health AI algorithms
Clinical teams including patients/caregivers in algorithm design
Consent forms specific to AI data use in healthcare
Ethics committee approvals for clinical AI deployment
Clear documentation that final diagnoses are made by humans
evidence_anti_signals: AI making diagnostic decisions without human review
